title: HTTP Services, Clients, Boundaries, Security, and Graceful Shutdown · Failure Repair Bay
summary: A small service accepts hostile input, calls a slow dependency, serves concurrent requests, and must shut down without losing work. Learners isolate plausible failures, compare competing hypotheses, and record cause-level repair evidence. Planned artifact: Repair a changed failure in a resilient secure HTTP service design and publish the reproduction, evidence, repair, and regression record. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evid…
objectives: Design Handler and ServeMux boundaries, method and path semantics, request limits, response status, headers, and streaming order.
Reuse configured clients and transports, set request contexts and timeouts, close bodies, bound reads, and classify status separately from transport errors.
Validate untrusted input, escape output for its sink, minimize authority and data, protect secrets, and set explicit server resource limits.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Inspect the failure record · Debug Http Handler Routing
steps.instruction:
debug case — Service request 4 has a hostile label, oversized body, slow dependency, duplicate header, and client cancellation during shutdown. Bound resources, validate and encode by sink, preserve stable responses, and drain owned work without leaking internals.

Inspect the Http Handler Routing trace for this debug case. Which record connects a precondition, observed state change, and boundary result?

Evidence target: Inspect the failure record · Debug Http Handler Routing.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for design handler and servemux boundaries, method and path semantics, request limits, response status, headers, and streaming order. For Inspect the failure record · Debug Http Handler Routing, keep Http Handler Routing connected to executable Go behavior evidence.
